summary: A heavy, dark spring fungus of older spruce forest whose rarity story is inseparable from habitat continuity, uneven fruiting, and strong observer bias in modern occurrence data.

fieldRecord.habitatNote: The linked photos show fruit bodies in moss and conifer litter, matching the species' usual early-spring appearance in older spruce-dominated forest.
fieldRecord.observationNote: EXIF-backed photo record from the linked image set. Running scripts/build_site_data.py will fill date, time and coordinates from the matching photos.

media.caption: Young fruit body of Witches cauldron in moss and conifer litter.
Close view showing the dark fertile disc and velvety brown outer surface.

hero.lede: Witches cauldron is one of the most memorable spring fungi in northern forests: dark, squat, gelatinous and unmistakably strange. It is also a strong example of why fungal conservation needs patience, because fruiting varies wildly between years and modern occurrence maps mix real habitat patterns with sudden bursts of observer attention.
hero.shortMorphology: Large barrel- to globe-shaped fruit bodies, often 5-12 cm wide, with a black-brown fertile disc and a velvety dark-brown outer surface; older fruit bodies flatten, wrinkle and collapse.
hero.returnReason: This species rewards repeat visits because known sites may fail to fruit in some years and then reappear, turning each spring into a real check rather than a static checklist exercise.

taxonomy.source: GBIF species match with Catalogue of Life checklistKey
taxonomy.resolvedAgainstDataset: Catalogue of Life checklist via GBIF species/match?checklistKey=7ddf754f-d193-4cc9-b351-99906754a03b

quickFacts.label: Primary habitat
quickFacts.value: Older spruce-dominated forest with moss and conifer litter

distribution.globalSummary: The strongest evidence base for Sarcosoma globosum lies in northern Europe and adjacent boreal regions, but the filtered GBIF layer also includes observations from North America. Those records are useful as an observation layer, not as a clean statement about continuous native range, and the strongest modern GBIF densities are in Russia and Finland rather than Sweden.
distribution.swedenSummary: Sweden remains one of the species' best-known conservation countries, but the filtered modern GBIF layer is tiny, with only 34 Swedish observations since 2000 under the project filters. That low count should be read together with the species' irregular fruiting and the strong role of observer culture in where records appear.

notes: The map shows aggregated GBIF occurrence density rather than individual points.
Filters are restricted to HUMAN_OBSERVATION and MACHINE_OBSERVATION records from year 2000 onwards and to records with occurrenceStatus = PRESENT.
The webpage renders this layer as a hex summary map.

distribution.samplingBiasNote: GBIF density here reflects changing fungal observation culture, reporting effort and digitization as much as biology. For this species in particular, a recent paper explicitly links rising observation counts to observation culture, so sparse hexes can mean under-reporting and dense hexes can reflect active search behaviour rather than a simple abundance pattern.

internationalContext.summary: Globally, Witches cauldron is a conspicuous but conservation-relevant forest fungus whose best-documented strongholds lie in northern Europe. The IUCN Red List assessment cited here treats it as Near Threatened in 2015, while recent literature emphasizes that the visible rise in observations can partly reflect changing fungal observation culture rather than a straightforward biological increase.
ecology: Usually described as a litter-associated terrestrial fungus in older spruce forest, although its exact trophic mode remains debated.
Fruits very early, often shortly after snowmelt, in moss and conifer litter on moist but usually well-drained ground.
Annual visibility is highly variable, so even established sites may need repeated visits across years.
Recent observation-culture work shows that rising record numbers do not automatically mean a biological increase; some of the signal comes from intensified search and reporting.
threats: Loss of old spruce-forest continuity through clear-cutting and stand replacement.
Decline in habitat quality where forest structure, moisture regime or litter conditions are altered.
Interpreting trends is difficult because fruiting is irregular and observation effort is uneven.

phenology.northernEurope: Typically fruits from shortly after snowmelt through spring, often from March to May.
phenology.note: Timing depends strongly on winter conditions, snow cover and local moisture.

swedishContext.summary: In Sweden, bombmurkla is a nationally important conservation fungus rather than just a curiosity. Artfakta, Dyntaxa and the local 2025 Red List data all align on the core story: the species is accepted taxonomically, known as bombmurkla, resident and reproducing, and assessed as EN because habitat quality and locality numbers are expected to decline. The long Swedish red-list documentation also makes clear that many historical localities have disappeared and that repeated site revisits are necessary because fruiting can fail in individual years.

redList2025.documentation: Artens levnadssätt är okänt. Den bildar fruktkroppar i mossa och barrförna på sandblandad mark i äldre blåbärs- och ängsgranskog, ofta vid moränryggar och i närheten av bäckdråg. Arten är välkänd och ofta eftersökt, med många nu försvunna lokaler. De senaste årens granbarkborreangrepp har försämrat statusen på flera av artens växtplatser i kärnområdet. Med förväntat ökade skogsskador i Götaland och Svealand bedöms hotnivån stiga. Sverige hyser en stor del av artens globala population. Antalet reproduktiva individer skattas till 17000 (11000-34000). Antalet har skattats genom att multiplicera tre faktorer: 1) Troliga antalet aktuella lokaler i landet (850). 2) Bedömt antal unika mycel/lokal (2). 3) En schablon för hur många fragmenterade enheter varje unikt mycel bedöms ge upphov till (10). Antalet lokalområden i landet skattas till 850 (550-1700). Utbredningsområdets storlek (EOO) överskrider gränsvärdet för rödlistning. Förekomstarean (AOO) skattas till 3300 (2250-6600) km². En minskning av populationen pågår eller förväntas ske. Minskningen avser förekomstarea, kvalitén på artens habitat och antalet lokalområden. Minskningstakten har uppgått till 30 % under de senaste 50 åren. Under de kommande 50 åren förväntas minskningstakten uppgå till 50 %. Under en tidsperiod om 50 år, som sträcker sig både bakåt och framåt i tiden, så bedöms minskningstakten uppgå till 40 %. Bedömningen baseras på minskad geografisk utbredning och/eller försämrad habitatkvalitet. De skattade värdena som bedömningen baserar sig på ligger alla inom intervallet för kategorin Starkt hotad (EN). Minskningstakten överstiger gränsvärdet för Starkt hotad (EN) enligt A-kriteriet. (A3c).

keyFacts: Artfakta/Dyntaxa taxon 1445 treats Sarcosoma globosum as accepted and bombmurkla as the recommended Swedish name.
The Swedish Red List 2025 data classify the species as EN under criterion A3c.
The local red-list documentation explicitly points to habitat-quality decline, lost localities and worsening pressure from forest damage.
Bombmurkla remains a strong revisit species because non-fruiting years can hide occupied sites.
